subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain how industrial growth, city life, new communication, and cultural changes affected life in the U.S. from the late 1800s to World War I.
Your child can describe how people worked to improve society and change unfair conditions during the Progressive Era.
Your child can explain what caused World War I, what happened because of it, and why the United States joined the war.
Your child understands how World War II changed life in Alabama, such as more women working, more jobs, rationing, use of military bases, drafting soldiers, and growing awareness of racial issues.
Your child can identify reasons why people moved to and settled in North America and what happened because of it.
Your child understands how American Indian cultures in North America were different based on where they lived, their resources, communities, economies, and beliefs.
Your child can compare old and current maps of Alabama to learn about its weather, land, waterways, transportation, politics, economy, land use, and population.
Your child understands how Alabama became a state and set up its government and constitutions.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Social Studies: United States and Alabama History — Industrialization, Wars, and Social Change
coherence_reason: The standards cover a broad array of historical topics including industrialization, wars, migration, indigenous cultures, statehood, and map interpretation, which are related by being historical but represent distinct subtopics.
split_topics: Industrialization and Social Change (late 1800s-WWI, Progressive Era, WWI, WWII)
Migration and Settlement in North America

subject: Science
sample_standards: Safety, Law and Ethics
Your child can describe when there is an emergency and who to ask for help.
Your child knows how to protect private health information.
Your child can identify healthy foods and activities that help keep the body strong.
Your child can find health information from home, school, and community sources.
Your child can write medical stories using correct spelling, grammar, and punctuation.
Your child knows what makes a good healthcare worker.
Your child can talk about what is right and wrong in health-related stories.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Health and Safety — Ethics, Emergencies, and Information
coherence_reason: The standards are related to health and safety but cover distinct subtopics such as emergency response, privacy, nutrition, healthcare professions, ethics, and health information.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child knows the Sun is the biggest object in the solar system and is mostly made of hydrogen and helium.
Your child knows that a planet moves around the Sun because of the Sun’s gravity pulling on it.
Your child knows the solar system includes Earth, the Moon, the Sun, eight other planets with their moons, and smaller objects like asteroids and comets.
Your child understands that air pressure from Earth’s atmosphere gets weaker as you go higher and pushes equally in all directions at any spot.
Earth's Place in the Universe
Your child can name objects and people related to Florida's space program.
Your child can name and identify three moon phases: full, half (quarter), and crescent.
Your child knows that the side of Earth facing the Sun has daylight.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Earth's Place in the Universe — Solar System and Celestial Phenomena
coherence_reason: The standards cover related topics about the solar system, celestial objects, and Earth's position and phenomena, but also include atmospheric pressure and specific space program knowledge, which are distinct subtopics.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can find and try out ways to fix simple computer or device problems.
Your child can find simple solutions to fix common computer hardware and software problems.
Your child can find and fix simple computer problems like power or connection issues during everyday use.
Hardware and software
Your child can find ways to fix common computer hardware and software problems.
Networks & System Design
Your child finds out why a product or system is not working properly.
Your child can figure out why a product or system is not working right.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Engineering and Technology — Troubleshooting Hardware and Software
coherence_reason: All standards focus on troubleshooting technology problems, with some describing computers specifically and others referring more generally to products or systems.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands that many plants have flowers and leaves.
Your child knows the main stages in the life cycles of common plants and animals.
Your child can compare how plants and animals change with the seasons in Florida and other parts of the country.
Your child can notice seasonal changes in some Florida plants, like flowers appearing or leaves changing color.
Your child notices how a common plant changes with the seasons.
Your child can recognize common behaviors of animals.
Your child understands that many flowering plants grow from their own seeds.
Your child can observe and describe materials that have changed, like leaves that have decayed.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Life Cycles and Seasonal Changes — Plants and Animals
coherence_reason: The standards address both life cycles and seasonal changes in plants and animals, which are related but distinct concepts that could be split.
split_topics: Life cycles of plants and animals
Seasonal changes in plants and animals

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will use pictures, music, or videos that are free to use, and will not copy things made by others without permission.
Your child will think of ways to make technology easier to use for people with different needs and preferences.
Your child can research STEM careers by exploring places like museums, libraries, online sites, and talking to experts.
Your child can use money skills to save and buy things they need or want.
Career Research
Your child can suggest ways to make technology easier and better for different users.
Your child can write, share, and publish work using technology tools.
Your child learns how to evaluate digital information sources carefully.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Technology and Society — Digital Citizenship and Career Awareness
coherence_reason: The standards address two related but distinct areas: digital citizenship/technology use and career/STEM exploration.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can make and use a model to show how waves have height and length and how waves can make things move.
Your child understands how waves work and how we use them in technology to send information.
Your child can investigate and explain that sounds are made by vibrating objects and that pitch depends on how fast they vibrate.
PS4.A: Wave Properties
Your child understands that waves are patterns made by moving water up and down, and that water mostly stays in place except near the shore.
Wave Properties
Your child can use models to show how waves have patterns and can make things move.
Your child learns about waves and how they carry information.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Waves — Properties and Applications
coherence_reason: All standards address wave properties and their applications, with minor variation between physical wave attributes (height, length, vibration) and information transmission.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child makes a model to show that we see objects because light reflects off them and enters our eyes.
Your child can show that we see things because light bounces off objects and enters our eyes.
Your child understands that we see objects when light bounces off them and enters our eyes.
Your child can build a model to show how light bounces off objects and into our eyes so we can see them.
Your child makes a model to show how light bounces off things and into our eyes so we can see.
Your child can plan and do experiments to observe how light passes through or is blocked by different materials.
Your child can find, understand, and share information about what light is and how it interacts with objects.
Your child can plan and do experiments to see how light travels to a mirror and bounces off at different angles.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Light — Reflection and Seeing Objects
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on light reflecting off objects to enable sight, but some include broader interactions of light (transmission, experiments on angles, what light is) rather than strictly reflection and vision.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can talk about how computers and technology have changed the world and how they are connected to different cultures.
Your child can talk about important computing technologies that changed the world and how culture affects and is affected by these technologies.
Your child learns how computers affect people and society locally and around the world.
Your child understands how devices like GPS or language translators help people do things they couldn't do before.
Your child can talk about current news stories that involve computer technology.
Your child can suggest ways to improve a computer technology by looking at how people use it.
Your child understands that what people need and want changes over time, and so do the technologies they ask for.
Your child can describe important computer technologies that changed the world and how they connect to culture.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Computing Technologies — Societal and Cultural Impacts
coherence_reason: All standards focus on how computing technologies have affected and been affected by society and culture, including historical impact, current events, and potential improvements.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can show how computer parts and programs work together to get things done.
Your child can explain how the inside and outside parts of a computer work together as a team.
Your child understands real-life problems with online safety and knows ways to keep personal information private.
Your child can show how information is split into small pieces, sent through the internet, and put back together at the end.
Your child can explain their opinion about a community issue and give a reason for it.
Your child understands how saving money and investing money are connected.
Your child can explain reasons why people borrow money and go into debt.
Your child can tell the difference between credit products and services.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Computers — Hardware, Software, and Data Transmission
coherence_reason: The standards mix computer systems, data transfer, online safety, civic participation, and financial literacy, which are clearly different topics.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child uses a model to show how animals get information through their senses, process it in their brain, and respond in different ways.
Your child explains how plants and animals have body parts inside and outside that help them survive, grow, behave, and reproduce.
Your child knows that many living things have special parts that help move materials around their bodies.
Your child understands that living things use feedback systems to keep their bodies stable and respond to changes around them.
Your child understands that kidneys remove waste from the blood and turn it into urine, which is stored in the bladder.
Your child knows the steps of digestion and how the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and colon each help break down food.
Your child understands how blood moves through the heart, lungs, and body, and how oxygen and carbon dioxide are exchanged in the lungs and tissues.
Your child learns about how living things are made of different parts and how those parts work together.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Structure and Function of Living Things — Body Systems and Responses
coherence_reason: All standards focus on the structure and function of living things, especially animal (including human) body systems and how they support survival, but there is some variation between senses, organ systems, and general structure/function concepts.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can organize and show data in charts or pictures to explain their thinking or prove a point.
Your child can use information to show or guess what causes what, make predictions, or explain ideas.
Your child can use math skills to understand how much borrowing costs.
Your child can make charts, graphs, and maps to organize data and show cause-and-effect relationships.
Your child can use calendars and timelines to learn about events from the past.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
Your child understands that different types or details of data need different amounts of space to store.
Your child can organize and show data in charts or pictures to explain relationships and support a point.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Data Analysis — Organizing and Representing Data
coherence_reason: While most standards address organizing and representing data, some focus on timelines/calendars (historical events) and one on borrowing costs (math/finance), indicating two distinct subtopics.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child thinks of and compares different ways patterns can be used to send information.
Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.
Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are.
Your child can divide circles and rectangles into equal parts like halves and thirds, describe these parts, and understand that equal parts can look different.
Your child can identify and draw shapes by their features, like how many angles or equal sides they have, including triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
Your child can notice and explain differences between objects or pictures.
Your child can group objects by how they look and feel, like their size, shape, color, texture, weight, or temperature.
Your child can compare things by looking at how big, what shape, color, feel, weight, hardness, smell, taste, and temperature they have.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Properties of Matter — Observing and Comparing Physical Properties
coherence_reason: The standards mix concepts about physical properties of objects (size, shape, color, etc.) with unrelated concepts about geometry (shapes, fractions, equal parts) and information patterns.
split_topics: Physical properties of matter (observation, comparison, sorting by features)
Geometry (identifying, drawing, and dividing shapes; fractions)

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain the choices they made while creating a computer program, using notes, presentations, or demonstrations.
Your child will take on different jobs, with help from the teacher, when working with classmates to design, build, and review computer programs.
Your child can break big problems into smaller steps to help create a computer program.
Your child can make computer programs that use steps in order, respond to events, repeat actions, and make decisions.
Your child will ask for ideas from different people to help make computer projects better.
Your child can find and fix mistakes in a computer program to make sure it works correctly.
Your child will plan computer programs by listening to others' ideas and thinking about what users want.
Your child will give credit to others when using or changing their computer programs.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Computer Science — Collaborative Programming and Problem Solving
coherence_reason: All standards focus on foundational programming concepts and collaborative practices in developing, improving, and reflecting on computer programs.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can use patterns in rocks and fossils found in Michigan to help explain how Michigan's land has changed over time.
Your child uses patterns in rocks and fossils to explain how a landscape has changed over time.
Your child can study how populations grow, spread out, and change over time.
Your child can name the three types of rocks: igneous (from melted rock), sedimentary (from pieces of other rocks and fossils), and metamorphic (changed by heat and pressure).
Your child can tell rocks apart from other things found on Earth's surface.
Your child understands that rocks are grouped by how they were made, like sedimentary rocks.
Your child can recognize common minerals like rock salt, talc, gold, and silver.
Your child can identify common minerals like rock salt, talc, gold, and silver.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Earth Materials — Rocks, Fossils, and Minerals
coherence_reason: Standards cover identification and classification of rocks and minerals, use of fossils and rocks to infer landscape changes, and also population changes which is a distinct concept.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can describe the world using maps and other geography tools.
Your child understands how regions help organize and describe Earth's surface.
Your child looks at maps and data to describe patterns in Earth’s features.
Your child can find important lines on maps and globes like the prime meridian, equator, Tropics of Capricorn and Cancer, International Date Line, and lines of latitude and longitude.
Your child can compare environmental patterns caused by Earth's air, land, water, and living things.
Your child can find important physical features like ocean currents, winds, forests, rivers, and mountains that affected exploration and settlement in the Americas.
Your child can locate all the continents on a map or globe.
Your child can show how farming, manufacturing, and services have different jobs and locations.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Geography — Maps, Regions, and Earth's Features
coherence_reason: The standards cover related topics about using maps and describing Earth's features, but also include distinct sub-concepts such as economic activities and environmental patterns that could be split.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child knows that cellular respiration breaks down food and oxygen to release energy needed for body functions like keeping warm.
Your child understands that animals eat plants or other animals for food.
Your child understands that animals cannot make their own food and must eat plants or other animals to live.
Your child understands that plants use sunlight to make food and animals eat plants or other animals.
Your child understands that plants use sunlight to make food, and animals eat plants or other animals for food.
Your child understands that animals eat plants or other animals to get their food.
Your child understands that animals eat food.
Your child understands that animals get energy by eating plants or other animals because they cannot make their own food.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Energy Flow in Ecosystems — How Plants and Animals Get Food and Energy
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on how plants make food and animals must eat plants or other animals for energy, but one standard includes cellular respiration, which is a related but deeper subtopic.
split_topics: How plants and animals obtain food

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can create and use models to show how things work or build a simple version of a solution to a problem.
Your child knows there are different kinds of models, like replicas or pictures.
Your child can identify models such as replicas, pictures, or animations.
Your child can build a simple model of something like a flower or the solar system and explain what it shows and what it doesn’t.
Your child can recognize different kinds of models, like replicas or pictures.
Your child can match a model that looks like a real object.
Your child can identify ideas shown in a simulation, like how ecosystems or predator-prey relationships work.
Modeling and Simulations
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Scientific Practices — Modeling and Simulations
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding, creating, and identifying different types of models and simulations, but some emphasize physical models while others include digital or conceptual models, resulting in minor variation.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can design, test, and improve a device that changes energy from one type to another.
Your child will ask questions and guess what will happen to energy when objects bump into each other.
Your child can watch and describe how energy moves from one place to another through sound, light, heat, and electricity.
Your child knows that during chemical reactions, atoms rearrange to make new substances with different properties.
Energy
Your child knows that metals share traits like conducting electricity and heat, and that some metals are pure while others are mixtures.
Your child understands that when water evaporates, it becomes vapor in the air and can turn back into liquid or ice when cooled.
Your child understands that all matter is made of atoms, which can join together to form molecules.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Physical Science — Energy and Matter
coherence_reason: The standards cluster around physical science but mix energy concepts (transfer, transformation) with matter concepts (atoms, molecules, chemical reactions, properties of metals, phase changes).

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will use evidence to explain how the speed of something relates to its energy.
Your child can explain that speed means how fast something travels a certain distance.
Your child can explore and explain that speed depends on how far something goes in a certain amount of time and that things can move at different speeds.
Your child can tell if something is moving fast or slow.
Your child can identify objects that move at different speeds.
Your child can identify things that move at different speeds.
Your child can tell if an object is moving fast or slow.
Your child knows that speed means how long it takes to go a certain distance.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Motion — Speed and Its Measurement
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding and explaining the concept of speed as it relates to motion, including how speed is measured and the comparison of fast and slow moving objects.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child observes or measures how weathering or erosion by water, ice, wind, or plants changes the land.
Your child knows water vapor moves through the air, forms fog or clouds, and falls as rain, hail, sleet, or snow.
Your child knows what causes different severe weather and what effects they have.
Your child understands how the ocean affects weather and how the water cycle influences weather patterns.
Identify ice as a solid.
Your child understands that wind and water can break down and move rocks.
Your child can name ways water is used when it is solid (ice) or liquid.
Your child can identify common changes that turn materials into new things, like burning, rusting, cooking, or decay.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Earth Systems — Weather, Erosion, and the Water Cycle
coherence_reason: The standards are related to Earth systems but cover three sub-concepts: weathering/erosion, properties of water/water cycle, and changes in materials, which could be split for clarity.
split_topics: Physical and Chemical Changes in Materials

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child comes up with and compares different ideas to lessen how natural Earth events impact people.
Your child understands how culture, like religion and food, helps define different regions.
Your child can explain how different factors affect whether objects, living things, or systems stay stable or change.
Your child understands what rights people have and what they should do as citizens in your community and state.
Your child understands how feelings and habits about money, saving, and work affect spending choices.
Your child knows that contracts are promises people must keep.
Your child knows who the leaders are in your town and state and what they do.
Your child can explain how living and nonliving things affect biodiversity and populations in ecosystems.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Earth's Processes — Reducing Impacts on Humans
coherence_reason: The standards cover a wide range of unrelated topics: earth science, culture, civics, economics, contracts, government, and ecology, with only two related to science and the rest belonging to social studies.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will gather information to explain that energy and fuels come from natural resources and that using them can affect the environment.
Identify sources of credit.
Your child can name resources found in Florida like water, phosphate, oil, limestone, silicon, wind, and sunlight.
Your child can name natural resources in Florida such as sunlight, water, and limestone.
Your child can identify natural resources in Florida, such as sunlight, water, and limestone.
Your child understands that some resources people use, like oil, cannot be replaced quickly.
Your child knows that some natural resources, like oil, cannot be replaced once used.
Your child knows about natural resources in Florida, like sunlight and water.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Natural Resources — Types, Uses, and Environmental Impact
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on identifying and understanding natural resources, their renewability, and environmental impact, though one unrelated standard about credit is present.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain how new scientific discoveries and inventions affect science and our daily lives.
Your child can use the ideas of supply and demand to understand buying goods and services.
Engineering Design
Your child can connect what they find to specific science questions.
Your child can answer questions about objects and actions related to science.
Your child understands how models and simulations help solve real-world science and engineering problems.
Your child can use thinking skills to solve real science and engineering problems.
Your child can explore how tools and technology help people see very small or very large things.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Science and Engineering Practices — Application and Impact
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on scientific practices and the impact of science and engineering, but one standard is about economic concepts unrelated to science.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.
Your child can come up with and compare different ways to reduce the effects of natural events on people and places in Michigan.
Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.
Your child can look at different ways to solve a problem with computer instructions and pick the best one.
Your child can judge a design or object by using specific rules or goals.
Your child can explain ways to reach money goals.
Your child can compare different financial services and products to see which fit their goals.
Your child can compare different ways to solve the same problem and choose the best one.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Engineering Design — Evaluating Solutions
coherence_reason: Standards address evaluating solutions to problems, but include both engineering design and unrelated topics like financial literacy and computer science.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.
Your child can carefully test and find mistakes in step-by-step instructions or plans.
Your child understands that people share information about science with others.
Your child can find information by watching themselves and others.
Your child knows scientists explain things based on evidence they find.
Your child understands that science helps us learn about the natural world around us.
Your child tries to answer science questions and supports their answers with evidence.
Your child knows that scientists collect evidence by watching carefully or measuring.
canonical_name: Grade 4 Science: Scientific Practices — Planning Investigations and Using Evidence
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on scientific investigation, planning fair tests, and using evidence, but a few broaden to general ideas about sharing science and learning about the natural world.
